questions.question:
What does this code print?

def greet():
    print("Hello!")

greet

options.explanation: That's what greet() (with parentheses) would print. Without the parentheses, you're just referencing the function object — nothing runs. The missing () is one of the most common beginner slip-ups.
Correct. Writing greet without parentheses just refers to the function object. It doesn't call it. To run the function and see Hello!, you need greet().
greet without quotes is a reference to the function, not a string. Python doesn't print variable names on their own — and even if it did, it wouldn't print Hello!.
This is valid Python — it just doesn't do what you might expect. Writing a name on its own line is legal; it simply evaluates the expression and discards the result.

questions.question:
What does this code print?

def add(a, b):
    print(a + b)

result = add(3, 4)
print(result)

options.explanation: 7 does appear — but it's printed from inside add(), not stored in result. Since add() has no return statement, result ends up as None, so a second value also prints.
Correct. add(3, 4) prints 7 as a side effect, then returns None (no return statement). That None is assigned to result, and the final print(result) displays it.
None does print — but not alone. The print(a + b) inside add() also fires and shows 7 before None appears.
No error here — the code runs fine. The surprise is in what it prints: 7 from inside the function, then None because add() never returns anything.

questions.question: A variable defined inside a function — what happens to it after the function returns?

options.text: It stays in memory and can be used later in the script.
options.explanation: Local variables don't persist. Once the function returns, they're gone. If you try to use them outside the function, you'll get a NameError.

options.text: It becomes a global variable automatically.
options.explanation: Python doesn't promote local variables to global scope on its own. For a value to be accessible outside the function, you have to explicitly return it.

options.text: It no longer exists — it was local to that function call.
options.explanation: Correct. Local variables live only for the duration of that function call. When the function returns, they're gone. To get a value out, return it and have the caller catch it.

options.text: It raises an error the next time the function is called.
options.explanation: No error — each call to a function gets its own fresh set of local variables. The previous call's locals are simply gone; they don't interfere with the next call.
